title: The Fastest Way to Know if Your Product Market Fit Is Real | Serval CEO, Jake Stauch
summary: Jake Stauch, CEO of Serval, argues that passionate early adopters are not proof of product-market fit and that real PMF manifests as customer pull rather than founder push. He advocates skipping formal customer interviews in favor of embedded relationships and cautions founders to distinguish genuine AI bets from convenient delusions.
writeup: Jake, co-founder and CEO of Servil, traces his path from seven years building Neuroplus (a neurofeedback headset for kids with ADHD) to founding an AI-native IT automation platform that closed a $75M Series B from Sequoia at a $1B valuation 18 months after founding. The central argument is the difference between delusional product-market fit (enthusiastic early adopters in a tiny addressable market) and real product-market fit (the tenor of customer conversations shifts from 'keep me posted' to 'how much does it cost, how do we start'), a distinction he could only recognize after witnessing the real thing at Verata. Servil's conviction through a year of zero traction rested on a platform theory: the full ITSM plus AI-native workflow builder plus access management stack had to exist before the product could be tested.
key_points: Neuroplus generated rabid fans and Kickstarter success yet never reached meaningful product-market fit because its market was too small and its early customers shared unusual traits with no natural gradient toward the broader market, a signal Jake only recognized in retrospect.

Real product-market fit became legible at Verata when a fumbled sales demo, where the rep got things wrong and the customer was confused, still ended with the customer saying 'sounds good, send me a quote for 30 cameras,' demonstrating that a great product sells through imperfect execution.

At Servil, Jake spent a year building with no market traction, sustaining conviction through a specific platform thesis: the product required combining full ITSM, AI-native workflow automation, and access management before any meaningful market test was possible, so 'one more feature' was a deliberate architecture bet rather than a delusion.

The inflection arrived in April or May of 2025 when, over a few-week period, customer conversations shifted almost overnight from 'interesting, keep us posted' to 'how much does it cost and what is the procurement process.'

Jake's operating discipline at Servil is five to six hours of customer calls per day with deliberate emotional neutrality, treating no single call as confirmation or disconfirmation, a correction to the pendulum swings between elation and despair that distorted his early-career judgment.

Servil automates the long tail of IT help desk work including password resets, onboarding and offboarding, just-in-time access requests, and policy questions, and positions itself as a broad enterprise automation platform rather than a narrow ticketing tool.

chapters.title: Neuroplus and the ADHD Hardware Pivot
Joining Verata and Witnessing Real PMF
